function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

The primary filing for this benchmark is the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026:

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Important:

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)
- X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

Question:
As of March 31, 2026, SpaceX disclosed approximately 9,600 broadband satellites supporting 10.3M Starlink subscribers and approximately 650 V1 Mobile satellites supporting 7.4M monthly unique satellite-to-mobile devices. Calculate the users-per-satellite ratio for each network and explain the utilization implications.
I'll analyze the SpaceX S-1 filing to find the satellite and subscriber data, then calculate the users-per-satellite ratios.
steps.reasoning_content:
The user is asking about SpaceX's S-1 filing dated May 20, 2026. I need to find information about:
1. ~9,600 broadband satellites supporting 10.3M Starlink subscribers
2. ~650 V1 Mobile satellites supporting 7.4M monthly unique satellite-to-mobile devices

Then calculate users-per-satellite ratio for each network and explain utilization implications.

Let me first parse the S-1 filing to find this information.

results.content: SUCCESS: Loaded portable assets for key: spacex_s1

arguments.prompt: Find the disclosure about approximately 9,600 broadband satellites supporting 10.3 million Starlink subscribers and approximately 650 V1 Mobile satellites supporting 7.4 million monthly unique satellite-to-mobile devices as of March 31, 2026 in {{spacex_s1}}

results.content:
Based on the description provided, here is the disclosure from the specified chunk:

communities worldwide. As of March 31, 2026, we had approximately 9,600 Starlink broadband and mobile satellites in Low-Earth Orbit, providing broadband connectivity to approximately 10.3 million Starlink Subscribers across 164 countries, territories, and other markets. We also provide satellite-to-mobile texting and over-the-top voice services to approximately 7.4 million monthly unique devices across approximately 30 countries.

• Starlink Consumer Broadband. We operate the world’s largest and most advanced space-based internet broadband service with median latency at approximately 25 milliseconds as of March 31, 2026. We provide fiber-like download speeds—at a median of 225 Mbps during peak hours for residential users as of March 31, 2026—and the technological capability to provide service everywhere on Earth, including the poles. This service quality is enabled by our vast network of approximately 9,600 Starlink broadband and mobile satellites in Low-Earth Orbit, which accounted for approximately 75% of all active maneuverable satellites in orbit as of March 31, 2026. We expect to commence deploying our next-generation V3 satellites, designed to offer one Tbps of downlink capacity per satellite, using Starship in the second half of 2026. We expect that a single Starship launch will be capable of deploying up to 60 V3 satellites to LEO, representing a potential twenty-fold increase in Starlink downlink capacity deployed relative to a Falcon 9 launch. As of March 31, 2026, we had approximately 10.3 million Starlink Subscribers, up approximately 105% from 5.0 million subscribers a year

*(Note: While the prompt mentions "approximately 650 V1 Mobile satellites," the specific chunk requested ("Continuing the Preamble's Starlink overview...") groups the broadband and mobile satellites together under the "approximately 9,600" figure and references the 7.4 million monthly unique devices. The specific breakdown of "approximately 650 V1 Mobile satellites" is detailed in a separate contiguous chunk regarding the company's satellite-to-mobile connectivity scale).*

steps.message: Let me search for more specific information about the V1 Mobile satellites breakdown.
steps.reasoning_content: I found the disclosure. Let me search for more details about the V1 Mobile satellites specifically.

arguments.prompt: Find the specific disclosure about approximately 650 V1 Mobile satellites and the satellite-to-mobile network details in {{spacex_s1}}

results.content:
Although the specific disclosure about the approximately 650 V1 Mobile satellites is not located in the chunk that defines key satellite constellation terminology and the SEC disclaimer, it is detailed in another section of the Prospectus Summary. 

The specific disclosure regarding the satellite-to-mobile network details and the approximately 650 V1 Mobile satellites states:

> "As of March 31, 2026, our dedicated satellite-to-mobile constellation of approximately 650 V1 Mobile satellites provides satellite-to-mobile data, over-the-top voice, and messaging services to approximately 7.4 million monthly unique devices across approximately 30 countries."

Additionally, the prospectus provides further context on the growth and capabilities of this network:

> "Since January 2025, we have grown our constellation from approximately 360 mobile V1 Mobile satellites to approximately 650 mobile V1 Mobile satellites. Through this constellation and in partnership with more than 30 mobile network operators, we provided data, over-the-top voice, and messaging services to approximately 7.4 million monthly unique devices across approximately 30 countries."

> "Our current capabilities under our “V1” constellation (consisting of approximately 650 V1 Mobile satellites in orbit) include light data, text messaging (SMS), and over-the-top voice services (e.g., WhatsApp and FaceTime)."

steps.message: I have the key disclosures. Now let me calculate the users-per-satellite ratios for each network.
steps.reasoning_content:
Now I have all the information I need. Let me calculate the users-per-satellite ratios:

1. Broadband network:
   - ~9,600 broadband satellites (note: this includes both broadband and mobile satellites per the disclosure, but the question asks about the broadband network specifically)
   - 10.3 million Starlink subscribers
   - Users per satellite = 10,300,000 / 9,600 = 1,072.92 users per satellite

Wait, let me re-read the question. The question says "approximately 9,600 broadband satellites supporting 10.3M Starlink subscribers and approximately 650 V1 Mobile satellites supporting 7.4M monthly unique satellite-to-mobile devices."

But the filing actually says "approximately 9,600 Starlink broadband and mobile satellites" - so the 9,600 figure includes both broadband and mobile satellites. However, the question is treating them as separate networks. Let me check this more carefully.

Actually, looking at the disclosure again:
- "approximately 9,600 Starlink broadband and mobile satellites in Low-Earth Orbit, providing broadband connectivity to approximately 10.3 million Starlink Subscribers"
- "our dedicated satellite-to-mobile constellation of approximately 650 V1 Mobile satellites provides satellite-to-mobile data, over-the-top voice, and messaging services to approximately 7.4 million monthly unique devices"

So the 9,600 figure includes both broadband and mobile satellites. The 650 V1 Mobile satellites are a subset of the 9,600. But the question is asking us to calculate users-per-satellite for each network.

Let me calculate:
1. Broadband network: 10.3M subscribers / 9,600 satellites = 1,072.92 users per satellite
2. V1 Mobile network: 7.4M monthly unique devices / 650 satellites = 11,384.62 devices per satellite

Wait, but the 9,600 includes the 650 V1 Mobile satellites. So if we want to calculate the broadband-only ratio, we should subtract the 650 mobile satellites from the 9,600 to get the broadband-only satellites: 9,600 - 650 = 8,950 broadband satellites.

But the question explicitly says "approximately 9,600 broadband satellites supporting 10.3M Starlink subscribers" - so the question is treating the 9,600 as broadband satellites. Let me follow the question's framing.

Actually, looking at the question more carefully, it says "approximately 9,600 broadband satellites" - but the filing says "approximately 9,600 Starlink broadband and mobile satellites". So the question is slightly mischaracterizing the disclosure. I should note this in my answer.

Let me calculate both ratios as the question asks:
1. Broadband: 10,300,000 / 9,600 = 1,072.92 users per satellite
2. V1 Mobile: 7,400,000 / 650 = 11,384.62 devices per satellite

The ratio of mobile to broadband users per satellite is approximately 10.6x.

Let me also note that the 9,600 figure includes the 650 V1 Mobile satellites, so the broadband-only ratio would be:
10,300,000 / (9,600 - 650) = 10,300,000 / 8,950 = 1,150.84 users per satellite

But I'll follow the question's framing and use 9,600 for the broadband calculation.

Let me do the calculations precisely.

steps.reasoning_content:
Now I have all the calculations I need. Let me also verify the disclosure by retrieving more context to make sure I'm interpreting it correctly.

Key findings:
1. Broadband network: 10.3M subscribers / 9,600 satellites = ~1,072.92 users per satellite
2. V1 Mobile network: 7.4M monthly unique devices / 650 satellites = ~11,384.62 devices per satellite
3. The mobile network has approximately 10.6x more users per satellite than the broadband network

Important caveat: The 9,600 figure actually includes both broadband AND mobile satellites per the filing. So if we exclude the 650 V1 Mobile satellites, the broadband-only ratio would be:
10.3M / (9,600 - 650) = 10.3M / 8,950 = ~1,150.84 users per satellite

But the question explicitly frames the 9,600 as "broadband satellites" so I'll use that framing while noting the caveat.

Let me also think about the utilization implications:
- The V1 Mobile network has a much higher users-per-satellite ratio (~11,385 vs ~1,073)
- This is because satellite-to-mobile services (texting, voice, light data) have much lower bandwidth requirements per user than broadband internet
- The V1 Mobile satellites are likely more efficiently utilized in terms of user count, but each user consumes far less capacity
- The broadband network requires more satellites per user because each subscriber demands high-bandwidth services (225 Mbps median download speeds)
- This suggests different capacity utilization models: broadband is capacity-constrained per user, while mobile is more about coverage and connection count

Let me submit the final answer.

arguments.final_result:
## Users-Per-Satellite Ratio Analysis — SpaceX Starlink Networks (as of March 31, 2026)

### Source Disclosure (SpaceX S-1, filed May 20, 2026)

Per the Prospectus Summary:
- **Broadband network:** "approximately 9,600 Starlink broadband and mobile satellites in Low-Earth Orbit, providing broadband connectivity to approximately 10.3 million Starlink Subscribers across 164 countries, territories, and other markets."
- **V1 Mobile network:** "our dedicated satellite-to-mobile constellation of approximately 650 V1 Mobile satellites provides satellite-to-mobile data, over-the-top voice, and messaging services to approximately 7.4 million monthly unique devices across approximately 30 countries."

### Calculations

**1. Broadband network (Starlink subscribers per satellite):**
- 10,300,000 subscribers ÷ 9,600 satellites ≈ **1,072.92 subscribers per satellite**

**2. V1 Mobile network (monthly unique devices per satellite):**
- 7,400,000 monthly unique devices ÷ 650 satellites ≈ **11,384.62 devices per satellite**

**Relative utilization:**
- 11,384.62 ÷ 1,072.92 ≈ **10.61× more users per satellite on the V1 Mobile network** than on the broadband network.

### Important Disclosure Caveat

The S-1's "approximately 9,600" figure is described as "Starlink broadband **and mobile** satellites," meaning the 650 V1 Mobile satellites are a subset of the 9,600 total. If one isolates broadband-only satellites (9,600 − 650 = 8,950), the implied broadband-only ratio would be:
- 10,300,000 ÷ 8,950 ≈ **1,150.84 subscribers per broadband satellite**

The question's framing treats 9,600 as the broadband denominator, so the headline ratio of ~1,072.92 is reported on that basis. Either way, the order-of-magnitude gap between the two networks is preserved.

### Utilization Implications

1. **Asymmetric per-satellite load.** The V1 Mobile constellation supports roughly an order of magnitude more users per satellite (~11,385 vs. ~1,073). This is not evidence of over-utilization on the mobile side; rather, it reflects fundamentally different service profiles.

2. **Bandwidth-per-user drives the gap.** Starlink broadband delivers median peak-hour download speeds of ~225 Mbps with ~25 ms median latency — a high-capacity, always-on, fiber-like service. Each subscriber consumes substantial downlink capacity, so the network needs many satellites per user. By contrast, V1 Mobile currently supports only "light data, text messaging (SMS), and over-the-top voice services" (e.g., WhatsApp, FaceTime) — low-bandwidth, intermittent, bursty traffic. A single V1 Mobile satellite can therefore serve many more devices because each device's capacity demand is a small fraction of a broadband subscriber's.

3. **Different capacity-constraint regimes.**
   - **Broadband:** capacity-constrained per user (each subscriber demands high sustained throughput). Utilization is measured in Mbps/Tbps per satellite, not user count. The planned V3 satellites (1 Tbps downlink each, ~60 per Starship launch) are explicitly aimed at relieving this per-user capacity constraint.
   - **V1 Mobile:** coverage- and connection-count-constrained. The relevant metric is reach (devices served, countries covered, MNO partnerships — 30+ operators) rather than aggregate throughput. The ~10.6× higher user-per-satellite ratio is consistent with a low-bit-rate, high-reach service model.

4. **Growth trajectory context.** The V1 Mobile constellation grew from ~360 to ~650 satellites since January 2025 (an ~81% increase) while supporting 7.4M devices — implying rapid device adoption per newly launched satellite. Starlink broadband subscribers grew ~105% year-over-year (from 5.0M to 10.3M) against a larger, more mature satellite base, indicating the broadband fleet is scaling to keep per-satellite subscriber load roughly stable while expanding total throughput.

5. **Interpretive caution.** Users-per-satellite is a coarse utilization proxy. It does not capture (a) per-satellite capacity (which differs materially between broadband and V1 Mobile satellites), (b) geographic distribution of demand, (c) orbital geometry/duty cycle, or (d) the fact that "monthly unique devices" on the mobile side is a different unit than "subscribers" on the broadband side (a subscriber is a paying account; a unique device may be a transient SMS/voice session). The ratio is therefore most useful as a directional indicator of service-model differences, not as a direct capacity-utilization comparison.

### Summary Table

| Network | Satellites | Users (as disclosed) | Users per Satellite |
|---|---|---|---|
| Starlink Broadband | ~9,600 | 10.3M subscribers | ~1,072.92 |
| V1 Mobile (satellite-to-mobile) | ~650 | 7.4M monthly unique devices | ~11,384.62 |
| Ratio (Mobile ÷ Broadband) | — | — | ~10.61× |

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm",
      "name": "SpaceX S-1 Registration Statement (filed May 20, 2026) — Prospectus Summary"
    }
  ]
}

results.content:
## Users-Per-Satellite Ratio Analysis — SpaceX Starlink Networks (as of March 31, 2026)

### Source Disclosure (SpaceX S-1, filed May 20, 2026)

Per the Prospectus Summary:
- **Broadband network:** "approximately 9,600 Starlink broadband and mobile satellites in Low-Earth Orbit, providing broadband connectivity to approximately 10.3 million Starlink Subscribers across 164 countries, territories, and other markets."
- **V1 Mobile network:** "our dedicated satellite-to-mobile constellation of approximately 650 V1 Mobile satellites provides satellite-to-mobile data, over-the-top voice, and messaging services to approximately 7.4 million monthly unique devices across approximately 30 countries."

### Calculations

**1. Broadband network (Starlink subscribers per satellite):**
- 10,300,000 subscribers ÷ 9,600 satellites ≈ **1,072.92 subscribers per satellite**

**2. V1 Mobile network (monthly unique devices per satellite):**
- 7,400,000 monthly unique devices ÷ 650 satellites ≈ **11,384.62 devices per satellite**

**Relative utilization:**
- 11,384.62 ÷ 1,072.92 ≈ **10.61× more users per satellite on the V1 Mobile network** than on the broadband network.

### Important Disclosure Caveat

The S-1's "approximately 9,600" figure is described as "Starlink broadband **and mobile** satellites," meaning the 650 V1 Mobile satellites are a subset of the 9,600 total. If one isolates broadband-only satellites (9,600 − 650 = 8,950), the implied broadband-only ratio would be:
- 10,300,000 ÷ 8,950 ≈ **1,150.84 subscribers per broadband satellite**

The question's framing treats 9,600 as the broadband denominator, so the headline ratio of ~1,072.92 is reported on that basis. Either way, the order-of-magnitude gap between the two networks is preserved.

### Utilization Implications

1. **Asymmetric per-satellite load.** The V1 Mobile constellation supports roughly an order of magnitude more users per satellite (~11,385 vs. ~1,073). This is not evidence of over-utilization on the mobile side; rather, it reflects fundamentally different service profiles.

2. **Bandwidth-per-user drives the gap.** Starlink broadband delivers median peak-hour download speeds of ~225 Mbps with ~25 ms median latency — a high-capacity, always-on, fiber-like service. Each subscriber consumes substantial downlink capacity, so the network needs many satellites per user. By contrast, V1 Mobile currently supports only "light data, text messaging (SMS), and over-the-top voice services" (e.g., WhatsApp, FaceTime) — low-bandwidth, intermittent, bursty traffic. A single V1 Mobile satellite can therefore serve many more devices because each device's capacity demand is a small fraction of a broadband subscriber's.

3. **Different capacity-constraint regimes.**
   - **Broadband:** capacity-constrained per user (each subscriber demands high sustained throughput). Utilization is measured in Mbps/Tbps per satellite, not user count. The planned V3 satellites (1 Tbps downlink each, ~60 per Starship launch) are explicitly aimed at relieving this per-user capacity constraint.
   - **V1 Mobile:** coverage- and connection-count-constrained. The relevant metric is reach (devices served, countries covered, MNO partnerships — 30+ operators) rather than aggregate throughput. The ~10.6× higher user-per-satellite ratio is consistent with a low-bit-rate, high-reach service model.

4. **Growth trajectory context.** The V1 Mobile constellation grew from ~360 to ~650 satellites since January 2025 (an ~81% increase) while supporting 7.4M devices — implying rapid device adoption per newly launched satellite. Starlink broadband subscribers grew ~105% year-over-year (from 5.0M to 10.3M) against a larger, more mature satellite base, indicating the broadband fleet is scaling to keep per-satellite subscriber load roughly stable while expanding total throughput.

5. **Interpretive caution.** Users-per-satellite is a coarse utilization proxy. It does not capture (a) per-satellite capacity (which differs materially between broadband and V1 Mobile satellites), (b) geographic distribution of demand, (c) orbital geometry/duty cycle, or (d) the fact that "monthly unique devices" on the mobile side is a different unit than "subscribers" on the broadband side (a subscriber is a paying account; a unique device may be a transient SMS/voice session). The ratio is therefore most useful as a directional indicator of service-model differences, not as a direct capacity-utilization comparison.

### Summary Table

| Network | Satellites | Users (as disclosed) | Users per Satellite |
|---|---|---|---|
| Starlink Broadband | ~9,600 | 10.3M subscribers | ~1,072.92 |
| V1 Mobile (satellite-to-mobile) | ~650 | 7.4M monthly unique devices | ~11,384.62 |
| Ratio (Mobile ÷ Broadband) | — | — | ~10.61× |
